The differential equation models the growth of the beetle population, combining logistic growth with a seasonal sinusoidal term. The general solution to the equation is:

$$
P(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\frac{K}{P_0} - 1\right)e^{-rt}} + A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right) \cdot \frac{K}{1 + \left(\frac{K}{P_0} - 1\right)e^{-rt}}
$$

This solution accounts for both the continuous growth of the population and the oscillating seasonal effect. The term $ A \sin\left(\frac{2\pi t}{12}\right) $ introduces periodic fluctuations in the population, which can affect the timing of the peaks.

The maximum population occurs when the sinusoidal term is at its peak, which happens at $ t = 6 $ months (mid-year). The sinusoidal term causes the population to peak twice a year, once in the spring and once in the fall. This means the beetle-watching activities should be planned around these peak times to capture the most abundant population.

The sinusoidal term also affects the timing of the peaks by shifting them slightly each year. This means the enthusiast should be aware of the seasonal variations and adjust their observations accordingly. The presence of the sinusoidal term introduces a natural rhythm to the population dynamics, which can make the beetle-watching experience more interesting and unpredictable.
